<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=15)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="N" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
<trait label="Timeless" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=291" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 142

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Achaekek](/Deities.aspx?ID=29), [Groetus](/Deities.aspx?ID=38), [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=83) ushers, [Pharasma](/Deities.aspx?ID=14)

**Native Inhabitants** [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (the dead), [psychopomps](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=83),

</column>

---

The Boneyard spans an impossibly tall and ever-growing spire of gleaming quintessence that rises up into the silver void of the Astral Plane. As the destination of the River of Souls, the Boneyard is where the souls of the mortal dead arrive for judgment and for Pharasma and her psychopomp servitors to direct them to their respective afterlives. Pharasma’s domain is separated into eight courts, each corresponding to one of the other planes of the Outer Sphere and collecting the souls due to that plane. Not every soul goes unchallenged, and proxies of gods and planes argue and debate over souls, with final arbitration conducted by Pharasma herself.

 While Pharasma rules absolutely within the Boneyard, she is not the only divinity there. The demigod psychopomp ushers dwell within the plane in service to her, and the deity Achaekek dwells below at the spire’s root. Looming high above as a skull-faced moon waits Groetus, the god of the end times, his orbit slowly decaying in minuscule iterations supposedly counting down to the last days of the cosmos.

 Pharasma’s courts and the surrounding Graveyard of Souls are not the only features atop the spire. Beyond the graveyard, the Spirelands manifest environments much like those of the varied Material Plane worlds, filled with the souls of neutral petitioners who have nowhere else to go as they are unclaimed by any gods and unaligned with any particular ethos.
name: Boneyard

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Elysium](/Planes.aspx?ID=16)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="CG" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 143

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Calistria](/Deities.aspx?ID=3), [Cayden Cailean](/Deities.aspx?ID=4), [Desna](/Deities.aspx?ID=5), [elven pantheon](/Deities.aspx?ID=197), [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10), [Gorum](/Deities.aspx?ID=7), giant pantheon, [Kofusachi](/Deities.aspx?ID=177), [Milani](/Deities.aspx?ID=43), [Nocticula](/Deities.aspx?ID=45)

**Native Inhabitants** [azatas](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=10), [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (the chosen)

</column>

---

Verdant, wild, and unrestrained by law, where passion and creation are fostered and rewarded, the plane of Elysium is a place of wild, idealized natural beauty. The so-called Promised Land and its inhabitants represent a wide variety of freely given benevolence, often willing to directly aid visitors but more often serving as inspirations and muses to foster positive change and self-realized success. Elysium's petitioners, known as the chosen, appear as idealized versions of their mortal selves, each pursuing their own self-determined actions and finding their own unique paths to join the ranks of the plane's celestials.

 Azatas—the plane's primary denizens—organize into fleeting, competitive courts, each rewarding heroism and creativity above all else. Elysium hosts a number of resident deities, including Calistria and the elven pantheon, Cayden Cailean, Desna, Gorum, and various empyreal lords. Visitors from across the planes are drawn to Elysium's Wandering City of Emerald Song, a mobile, impermanent, and ever-changing city of [lillend azatas](/Monsters.aspx?ID=36) organized by their princess, Anduarine the Muse, filled with art, craft, revelry, song, and trade.
name: Elysium

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Heaven](/Planes.aspx?ID=17)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="LG" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 143

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [dwarven pantheon](/Deities.aspx?ID=196), Easivra, [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10), [Erastil](/Deities.aspx?ID=6), [Iomedae](/Deities.aspx?ID=9), [Shizuru](/Deities.aspx?ID=47), [Torag](/Deities.aspx?ID=18), [Tsukiyo](/Deities.aspx?ID=48)

**Native Inhabitants** [archons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=9), [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (the elect)

</column>

---

The great mountain of Heaven is the realm of structured benevolence made manifest. Organized into seven tiers, the mountain’s solid appearance is actually malleable, making way for a vast assemblage of varied environments to accommodate both its own celestials and the souls who migrate there. Devoted to defending the innocent and crusading against the wicked, Heaven’s archons marshal into vast armies, commanded by their own empyreal lords. [Angels](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=3) collaborate with the archon legions but typically act in more direct service to Heaven’s resident deities, with Iomedae, Erastil, and Torag the most prominent among them.

 Petitioners known as the elect manifest at the mountain’s base, and their subsequent climb up the seemingly endless, unreachable heights is both a literal and figurative journey. Progress is about personal growth and spiritual purification as the petitioners grow more and more attuned to the plane. Likewise, progress from one layer to the next is often impossible without permission from the archons or spiritual alignment with Heaven itself. Most visitors arrive at the city of Heaven’s Shore, a place open to both traders and to pilgrims of good intent. Access beyond its heavily guarded walls is difficult, to say nothing of scaling the mountain. At its pinnacle is the Garden, Heaven’s ultimate layer. Unattended and empty, the Garden is a beautiful, transcendent mystery even to its resident deities, and the source of the plane’s call of self-perfection to its petitioners.
name: Heaven

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Hell](/Planes.aspx?ID=18)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="LE" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 144

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [archdevils](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=4), [Asmodeus](/Deities.aspx?ID=2), [Dahak](/Deities.aspx?ID=150), [Erecura](/Deities.aspx?ID=151), [General Susumu](/Deities.aspx?ID=176), infernal dukes, Minderhal, [Yaezhing](/Deities.aspx?ID=184), Zursvaater, [queens of the night](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=14)

**Native Inhabitants** [devils](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=30), [hellhounds](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=60), [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (the damned)

</column>

---

Hell is the realm of devils, the multiversal seat of tyranny and malignant law, and the divine domain of Asmodeus, the Prince of Darkness. Here every act is authorized, calculated, recorded, and set like perfectly ordered clockwork within a vast machine driven on methodical suffering and greased with pain and purification. The nine inverted layers of Hell violently oppose the surrounding fabric of the Maelstrom, each layer shaped to reflect the nature of its ruling archdevil. Avernus's volcanic wastes, ruled by [Barbatos](/Deities.aspx?ID=64), are the marshaling place of Hell's armies, and where newly damned souls are shackled and shuffled off to their assigned torment. [Dispater's](/Deities.aspx?ID=66) layer of Dis is a great iron city: brutal, beautiful, and terrible. The layer of Erebus comprises both the sewers of Dis and the vaults and treasuries of Hell, ruled by [Mammon](/Deities.aspx?ID=68), a great genius loci embodied by the very wealth locked within Hell's coffers. Ruled by [Belial](/Deities.aspx?ID=65), the layer of Phlegethon hosts Hell's forges, while [Geryon's](/Deities.aspx?ID=67) watery realm of Stygia houses Hell's libraries. [Moloch's](/Deities.aspx?ID=70) smoldering, ash-draped forest realm of Malebolge is the training site of the infernal armies, and [Baalzebul's](/Deities.aspx?ID=63) frozen layer of Cocytus torments the imprisoned, starving damned. [Mephistopheles](/Deities.aspx?ID=69) rules the layer of Caina, a realm of cages and torture suspended above a pit of hungry darkness, while at the very bottom of Hell's infinite pit, Asmodeus rules unquestioned from his throne in Nessus.
name: Hell

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=19)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="CN" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
<trait label="Metamorphic" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=282" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 144

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Besmara](/Deities.aspx?ID=32), [Hanspur](/Deities.aspx?ID=154), [Hei Feng](/Deities.aspx?ID=40), [Naderi](/Deities.aspx?ID=157), [Nalinivati](/Deities.aspx?ID=181), [Nethys](/Deities.aspx?ID=12), orc pantheon, protean lords, [Sivanah](/Deities.aspx?ID=46), Speakers of the Depths, [Sun Wukong](/Deities.aspx?ID=183), [Yamatsumi](/Deities.aspx?ID=185), [Ydersius](/Deities.aspx?ID=211)

**Native Inhabitants** [proteans](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=82), [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (the shapeless)

</column>

---

The Maelstrom, infinite and ancient, spawned the other Outer Planes in cosmological prehistory and surrounds them like a vast metaphorical—and at times literal—ocean of raw, chaotic quintessence. Where the Maelstrom borders these other planes, its structure takes on their characteristics, albeit in an unpredictable, chaotic fashion. Beyond these so-called Borderlands, however, the Maelstrom reverts to its true nature, the Cerulean Void: a trackless, liquid infinity devoid of stability and permanence where serpentine proteans create and destroy with profound frivolity. The proteans are paradoxically organized into discrete choruses, each with its own philosophy and goals in service to the Maelstrom. Swirling with oddities and wild magic, bereft of laws and structure, the Maelstrom also serves as a conduit between the other Outer Planes utilized by armies of extraplanar beings, hordes of [demons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=28) spilling forth from the Abyss, and the innumerable protean choruses seeking to return the rest of the planes to the true freedom of the chaos from which they emerged. Sufficiently powerful magic can stabilize the chaos for a time, allowing for the creation of demiplanes and stable islands. The massive planar trade city of Galisemni is most prominent among these, drifting through the chaos.
name: Maelstrom

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Nirvana](/Planes.aspx?ID=20)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="NG" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 144

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Daikitsu](/Deities.aspx?ID=174), [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10), [Gruhastha](/Deities.aspx?ID=39), [Kazutal](/Deities.aspx?ID=41), [Kurgess](/Deities.aspx?ID=42), [Qi Zhong](/Deities.aspx?ID=182), [Sarenrae](/Deities.aspx?ID=16), [Shelyn](/Deities.aspx?ID=17)

**Native Inhabitants** [angels](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=3), [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (the cleansed)

</column>

---

The pastoral paradise of Nirvana is the realm of purest good, a plane that promises sanctuary to the weary and enlightenment and transcendence to those who seek it out. Filled with beautiful wilderness of all types in perfect harmony with its occupants, Nirvana’s wilds are home to angels and others. The plane’s petitioners, known as the cleansed, most often take the forms of glorified, sapient wild animals, though many eventually leave their carefree existence to aid others, ascending to assist the benevolent works of angels. Devoted to guiding and assisting mortals, angels are often charged with delivering important messages that reflect the will of benevolent deities.

 Various divinities make their home amid Nirvana’s wilds, including a host of empyreal lords and, most prominently, Sarenrae and Shelyn. While the great cities of High Ninshabur and Llourith welcome visitors, most mortals visiting Nirvana witness the plane’s beauty and supernatural feeling of peace but rarely encounter many of its residents, as the plane itself protectively hides much of its contents from any but the most selfless and pure of heart. The plane’s hinterlands hint at hidden mysteries, including legends that the plane shelters the vanished heroes of innumerable mortal worlds, peacefully sleeping until their peoples’ times of greatest need.
name: Nirvana

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Dimension of Time](/Planes.aspx?ID=21)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="N" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
<trait label="Erratic" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=275" />
<trait label="Static" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=288" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 145

**Category** Dimensions

**Divinities** Tawil at’Umr, [Yog-Sothoth](/Deities.aspx?ID=166)

**Native Inhabitants** [hounds of Tindalos](/Monsters.aspx?ID=702), [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (the unbound)

</column>

---

Among the most obscure planes of existence, the Dimension of Time is virtually impossible to access by conventional methods of planar travel. Instead, travelers much perform complex and difficult rituals described within the pages of such monstrously rare tomes as the _Necronomicon_ and the _Book of Serpents, Ash, and Acorns: Shadows of What Was and Will Be_. Compounding the plane’s obscurity, each traveler’s experience seems to be unique, and most who attempt the journey never return. Every manner of magic that interacts with the plane invariably draws the attention of creatures such as the hounds of Tindalos. The proper flow and continuity of time seems to self-correct for any attempted meddling, often with violence.

 Those few who have gained entry and returned describe the plane as a swirling storm of blurred images, the composite of millions of interwoven timelines around their own, with a single doorway allowing access to one’s own past. Travelers appear as transparent images of themselves, but the plane seems averse to any attempt to alter the past, no matter the means or the intention. Such attempts often end in the offending individual erased from existence or trapped within closed time loops to limit the damage they inflict upon the overall flow of time.

 Supposedly, like an eye in a storm, at the dimension’s heart is the legendary realm of Stethelos and its terrible occupant Tawil at’Umr. Tales of this realm speak only of a great city, a vast green meadow, and the rushing sound of an ocean emptying over an immense and terrible waterfall.
name: Dimension of Time

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Dreamlands](/Planes.aspx?ID=22)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="N" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
<trait label="Flowing" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=277" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Gamemastery Guide](/Sources.aspx?ID=22) pg. 145

**Category** Dimensions

**Divinities** Elder Mythos pantheon

**Native Inhabitants** [animate dreams](/Monsters.aspx?ID=546), [Denizens of Leng](/Monsters.aspx?ID=604), [Leng spiders](/Monsters.aspx?ID=713), [petitioners](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (dreamers)

</column>

---

Created and sustained by the collective dreams of sleeping mortals, the Dreamlands (also called the Dimension of Dreams) overlays the Ethereal Plane. When a creature dreams, regardless of the location of their physical body, they interact directly with the Dreamlands. Sleeping creatures cast themselves into the plane in idealized avatars known as lucid bodies, and their dreamscapes are immune to outside entry by standard magic such as [_plane shift_](/Spells.aspx?ID=222), requiring obscure, more specialized spells to access. The dreamers of each mortal world generate a cluster of dreamscapes, like drifting bubbles atop a deeper ocean of permanent dreams formed from the collective mass of slumbering desires, dreams of especially powerful dreamers, and the dreams of ancient, obscure entities—including the gods of the Elder Mythos. While most dreamscapes are safe, travelers in the deep, permanent Dreamlands face living, animate dreams and the predation of night hags from the Ethereal Plane, as well as stranger beings spawned within the core itself.

 Some scholars postulate a distinct region within the dimension for nightmares, much as the more stable inner portions of the dimension exist separately from the transient, ever-forming and evaporating mortal dreamscapes at its edges. Others conflate this nightmare region with the demiplane of Leng, while still others dismiss this notion but speculate that Leng is somehow accessible to dreamers who intentionally seek it out.
name: Dreamlands

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[The Harrow Court](/Planes.aspx?ID=23)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="N" url="/Rules.aspx?ID=95" />
<trait label="Finite" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=276" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [Pathfinder #190: The Choosing](/Sources.aspx?ID=198) pg. 71

**Category** Dimensions

**Divinities** none

**Native Inhabitants** simulacra

</column>

---

The Harrow Court appears as an idyllic region that could have been plucked from the heart of Avistan. At the demiplane's center, a fortress known as Harrowheart sits atop a hill, which is in turn surrounded by a village and farmlands. To the north, the farmlands give way to barren deserts, while to the south they decline into a swamp. A vast lake lies to the east, and a dense forest grows in the west, while all around, towering mountains surround the realm, giving it the appearance of being nestled in a single vast valley. The sun rises in the east every morning at 6:00 am and sets in the west every evening at 6:00 pm, splitting time into equal periods of twelve hour days and nights. The sky above appears normal (complete with moon and stars at night) but has an impassible barrier—the edge of the demiplane—at an altitude of 3 miles.
name: The Harrow Court

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Plane of Metal](/Planes.aspx?ID=24)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Metal" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=505" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 176

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [elemental lords of metal](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8)

**Native Inhabitants** [metal elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=343), [shades](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (metal pneuma), [taloses](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=58), [zuhras](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=50)

</column>

---

After being cut off since prehistory, the Plane of Metal recently returned to the planar cosmology. Still resuming its ancient form, it wedges between the Plane of Earth and Plane of Water, vast in scope but still expanding. Metal of every kind (liquid and solid), decaying cityscapes, and intense magnetic fields make up the chaotic structure of the plane. Creatures reflect the jumble of forces that make their home. Many have fluid forms of flowing metal or inhabit clouds of rust. The genies of the plane, zuhras, put on bold performances keyed into the plane’s dissonance.

The elemental lords of metal embody the slow disintegration of their home. After an invasion by forces from the Plane of Earth before their plane was sealed off, the lords were dispirited and offered no clear vision for what their plane could be in the absence of the other planes of the Inner Sphere. Laudinmio, the Sovereign of Alchemy, remains in slumber, while Ferrumnestra, the Lady of Rust, treads under the weight of deep despair.
name: Plane of Metal

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Plane of Wood](/Planes.aspx?ID=25)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Subjective Gravity" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=705" />
<trait label="Wood" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=508" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 176

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [elemental lords of wood](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8)

**Native Inhabitants** [ardandes](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=57), [forest dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=227), [kizidhars](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=50), [kodama](/Monsters.aspx?ID=1202), [shades](/Monsters.aspx?ID=758) (wood pneuma), [wood elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=345)

</column>

---

The Plane of Wood recently returned to the planar cosmology after an absence of eons. Still rebuilding to its ancient splendor, it climbs between the Plane of Water and Plane of Air. Fractal tree growth, eternally tended and shaped by the plane’s inhabitants, stretches across the plane with dizzying symmetry. Creatures either enforce and augment this ordered growth or exist as a byproduct thereof. Some drop from the trees as discarded seeds given fresh life, while others chisel or cultivate creatures’ natural beauty to suit their aesthetic tastes. The genies of the plane, kizidhars, are artisans in this field.

The elemental lords of wood embody the often-paradoxical rigidity and pliancy of their element. When other planes of the Inner Sphere came under threat, the lords acted swiftly to seal off their home, protecting the plane and its residents—yet even in this, they’re said to have disagreed, and the two haven’t spoken in millennia. Shumunue, the Carved Lady of Mimicry, desperately searches for a cure for her great ailment, while Verilorn, Custodian of Oak and Ash, worries that ending the plane’s long isolation will lead to its ruin.
name: Plane of Wood

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[The Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26)</title>

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 174

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** Dominion of the Black, Gozreh

**Native Inhabitants** [dwarves](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=59), [elves](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=60), [halflings](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=63), [humans](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=64), [gnomes](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=61), [goblins](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=62), and countless other ancestries

</column>

---

The Universe is the prosaic realm and the home of mortal life. Innumerable galaxies play host to countless stars and their planets, each housing unique settings for any campaign, with Golarion as the classic example. Other worlds of note like Castrovel, Akiton, Triaxus, and Eox exist within Golarion’s own solar system, and then beyond this, orbiting other distant stars or in other galaxies still, worlds such as Androffa and even Earth swirl within the Universe’s vast and silent void.

Yet, for all the profound wonder and diversity of life that the Universe houses, strange alien gods and the sinister collective of malevolent beings known as the [Dominion of the Black](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=394) lurk in the dark places between the stars known as the Dark Tapestry.

The Universe serves as the destination for pre-incarnate souls, each mortal life born, living, and dying before sending its spirit toward the [planes of the Outer Sphere](/Rules.aspx?ID=3024) for judgment. The Universe is likewise the focus of the gods, each of whom is invested in fostering their own particular portfolio among mortal worshippers and the material world at large.
name: The Universe

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Creation's Forge](/Planes.aspx?ID=27)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Subjective Gravity" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=705" />
<trait label="Unbounded" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=720" />
<trait label="Vitality" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=509" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 175

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** none

**Native Inhabitants** [jyotis](/Monsters.aspx?ID=708), shades (enlightened)

</column>

---

Creation’s Forge is at once the source of life-sustaining vitality energy, the origin of all pre-incarnate mortal souls, and paradoxically the most innately hostile of all of the planes. While vitality energy is deadly to undead and beneficial to living beings, such is the intensity of the plane that unmitigated exposure ultimately incinerates any extraplanar beings without sufficient magical protection. The most apt comparison for the plane’s interior is that of the heart of a burning star. Indeed, the stars of [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26) each house natural portals to Creation’s Forge within their glowing, potent cores to foster the movement of pre-incarnate souls in their first steps in the great cycle of life and death.

Brilliant and blinding, the plane’s interior is sparsely populated, and the resident phoenix-kin [jyotis](/Monsters.aspx?ID=708) are intensely xenophobic. Dwelling in glimmering, radiant crystalline cities such as Arudrellisiir, they view themselves as gardeners and guardians of souls spawned from their realm’s burning quintessence. Intensely distrustful of gods and their servitors, jyotis can nonetheless be bargained with, and they’ve frequently taken into their custodianship any number of artifacts and imprisoned beings considered too dangerous to house on any other plane.
name: Creation's Forge

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[The Void](/Planes.aspx?ID=28)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Subjective Gravity" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=705" />
<trait label="Unbounded" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=720" />
<trait label="Void" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=510" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 175

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** none

**Native Inhabitants** [sceaduinars](/Monsters.aspx?ID=794), [wraiths](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=164), and other [undead](/Traits.aspx?ID=722)

</column>

---

The vast nothingness of the Void is a merciless, lightless expanse of manifest destruction and nihilism. Sapping and consuming the life force of any living creature exposed to its energies, it corrodes and disintegrates material objects to rubble, then dust, and then nothing at all, yet the Void contains its own form of anti-life. At their densest concentration, the plane’s energies aggregate into bizarre, black crystalline snowflake structures. These irregularities spontaneously generate the plane’s resident [sceaduinars](/Monsters.aspx?ID=794). Dwelling in beautiful, deadly cities drifting in the vacuous darkness, these so-called void raptors are incapable of true creation and blame this flaw on some ancient betrayal by their rivals in [Creation's Forge](/Planes.aspx?ID=27). Sceaduinars react violently not only toward creatures sustained by [vitality](/Traits.aspx?ID=509) energy, but also toward [undead](/Traits.aspx?ID=722), whom they view as unnatural parasites unworthy of their plane’s energies.

The black depths swarm with undead, creatures doomed to a mockery of life by the interaction of their souls with the plane’s entropic energy. For all the horrors posed by the Void’s environment, inhabitants, and undead victims, its depths nevertheless hide rare refuges for planar travelers. Malikar's Keep offers shelter, for a price, to those willing to bargain with its titular lich master. Elsewhere, the depths hide entire lost worlds, such as Fallen Duromak, and deadly mysteries like the planet-sized, undead-entrapping glass sphere, Eternity's Doorstep.
name: The Void

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Plane of Air](/Planes.aspx?ID=29)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Air" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=527" />
<trait label="Subjective Gravity" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=705" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 175

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [elemental lords of air](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8)

**Native Inhabitants** [air elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=408), [cloud dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=176), [jaathooms](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3003), shades (air pneuma)

</column>

---

The Plane of Air, innermost of the Elemental Planes, is a vast realm of wind, storms, and skies. Illuminated by great artificial globes of flame and distant starlight from [the material universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), the plane is populated by [air elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=408), [dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=176), [air scamps](/Monsters.aspx?ID=2985), and a great empire of [jaathoom genies](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3003). Though mostly clouds and empty skies, the plane isn’t entirely bereft of solid ground, including rock and ice created by the residents or dragged into their realm from the distant [Plane of Earth](/Planes.aspx?ID=30) or neighboring [Plane of Water](/Planes.aspx?ID=32). The returning pockets of the [Plane of Wood](/Planes.aspx?ID=25) have let loose giant pieces of driftwood into the plane as well. Bizarre, drifting spheres of brass and iron make for the most convenient navigational aids; the former housing the cities of the vast Jaathoom Empire, the latter almost entirely abandoned and shunned by the plane’s inhabitants, who believe them cursed, entrapping forgotten, ancient enemies who once ravaged the plane.

The jaathooms rule from their shining capital city of Armun Kelisk, built atop a series of seven floating islands. Their vast trade network crisscrosses the skies and ventures to other planes, kept aloft by natural and magical flight, including great [airships](/Vehicles.aspx?ID=57) that allow visitors to quickly and safely traverse the skies. The jaathooms are welcoming and gracious hosts to extraplanar travelers and adventurers, a perspective not shared by [Hshurha the Duchess of All Winds](/Deities.aspx?ID=103), one of the [elemental lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8) of air.
name: Plane of Air

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Plane of Earth](/Planes.aspx?ID=30)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Earth" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=585" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 175

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [elemental lords of earth](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8), [Nivi Rhombodazzle](/Deities.aspx?ID=44)

**Native Inhabitants** [crystal dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=177), [earth elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=409), [jabalis](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3004), shades (earth pneuma)

</column>

---

A great and rocky shell situated between the [Plane of Fire](/Planes.aspx?ID=31) and [Plane of Water](/Planes.aspx?ID=32), the Eternal Delve hosts a unique and varied ecology of creatures at home in its rocky depths. Far from an endless, solid expanse, the [Plane of Earth](/Planes.aspx?ID=30) is riddled with great caverns and cave systems, excavated artificial vaults, vast crystalline geodes, and underground oceans and springs of magma where it borders its neighboring planes. Housing untold riches in gemstones and veins of precious metals, the Plane of Earth is an attractive setting for planar travelers seeking wealth and willing to risk danger and the wrath of [elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=409), [jabalis](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3004), and other inhabitants who resent the plunder of their home.

While elementals have little organized society, the jabali genies’ Peerless Empire rules much of the plane from its capital, the Opaline Vault—a rainbow-lit city within a 30-mile-wide geode cavern. They brook little dissent, and their rule can be harsh. They’re downright welcoming, however, compared to [Ayrzul the Fossilized King](/Deities.aspx?ID=102), the hated [elemental lord](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8) of earth who rules a great expanse of rock from his radiation-poisoned domain, the Blistering Labyrinth.
name: Plane of Earth

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Plane of Fire](/Planes.aspx?ID=31)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Fire" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=604" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 176

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [elemental lords of fire](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8), Feronia

**Native Inhabitants** [fire elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=410), [ifrits](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3006), [magma dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=178), [munsahirs](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=342), shades (fire pneuma)

</column>

---

Like a great, gleaming ball of flame situated at the heart of the [Astral Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=33), the Plane of Fire is the outermost plane of the [Inner Sphere](/Rules.aspx?ID=3022). A perpetual ocean of fire with skies of smoke, storms of raining cinders, and lakes and rivers of magma flowing along its border with the [Elemental Plane of Earth](/Planes.aspx?ID=30), the plane is incredibly hostile to those unprepared for its natural hazards. Yet, it houses one of the most well-known and traversed cities in all the planes: Medina Mudii’a, capital of the Dominion of Flame. Floating above a sea of fire upon a great hemisphere of magical brass, Medina Mudii’a is a monument both to [ifrits’](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3006) cosmopolitan grandeur and tyranny, the latter embodied by the grand sultana of the ifrits. Outside of the mercantile districts and the palaces and temples of the fire genies, the city houses a vast oppressed underclass, including creatures from other planes.

The ifrit domain isn’t absolute, and elsewhere in the plane are nations of [fire scamps](/Monsters.aspx?ID=2987) and the remains of an ancient [munsahir](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=342) empire. While they don’t form any cohesive, organized nations, the plane’s [fire elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=410) are ruled over by the plane’s most powerful entity, [Ymeri the Queen of the Inferno](/Deities.aspx?ID=108), the warlike [elemental lord](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8) of fire whose reign has gone unquestioned since the imprisonment of her rival [Atreia](/Deities.aspx?ID=101) eons ago.
name: Plane of Fire

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Plane of Water](/Planes.aspx?ID=32)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Subjective Gravity" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=705" />
<trait label="Water" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=732" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 176

**Category** Inner Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [elemental lords of water](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8)

**Native Inhabitants** [brine dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=175), [faydhaans](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3005), shades (water pneuma), [water elementals](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=412)

</column>

---

Beyond the skies of the [Plane of Air](/Planes.aspx?ID=29), the roots of the newly regrown [Plane of Wood](/Planes.aspx?ID=25) are nurtured by the vast, spherical, liquid shell of the Plane of Water. Its nearly limitless stretches of saline, fresh, and brackish seas teem with all manner of oceanic life, lit by submerged suns and bioluminescence before descending into the black, benthic depths where the plane borders the [Plane of Earth](/Planes.aspx?ID=30). While it’s perfectly amenable to water-breathing creatures, air-breathing travelers must provide their own supply of air or magical means to breathe while on the plane. Bubbles of breathable atmosphere are relatively rare and securely guarded, anchored over places of trade and commerce with outsiders, such as the great city of Vialesk, founded by immigrant [undines](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=37). The plane’s oceans, dotted by vast forests of kelp, magical currents, and strange phenomena, play host to empires of [merfolk](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=443), predatory and expansionist [sea devils](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=88), and the holdings of the plane’s great [brine dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=175).

While [faydhaans](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3005) once claimed nearly absolute dominion over the plane of their origin, their empire long ago fell into deterioration and disunity. Their ravaged cities sank into the depths, and their present-day holdings remain a shadow of their former grandeur. [Kelizandri the Brackish Emperor](/Deities.aspx?ID=104), the prideful [elemental lord](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=8) of water and cause of the genies’ ruin, rules the dark, saline depths, feasting on all rivals and hoarding the vast, stolen wealth of the fallen faydhaan cities—but the return of his counterpart [Lysianassa, Empress of the Torrent](/Deities.aspx?ID=105), now threatens his hold on the plane. Unlike other subjective gravity planes, on the Plane of Water, a creature moves based on its [swim Speed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2351) and must use actions to [Swim](/Actions.aspx?ID=2381) if it doesn’t have one.
name: Plane of Water

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Astral Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=33)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Subjective Gravity" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=705" />
<trait label="Timeless" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=713" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 177

**Category** Transitive Planes

**Divinities** [Alseta](/Deities.aspx?ID=30), [Apsu](/Deities.aspx?ID=149)

**Native Inhabitants** shades (untethered), [shining children](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3190)

</column>

---

The Silver Sea surrounds the [planes of the Inner Sphere](/Rules.aspx?ID=3022), separating them from those of the [Outer Sphere](/Rules.aspx?ID=3024). The Astral Plane provides the backdrop against which the River of Souls flows from [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), ushering departed spirits toward the [Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=39) for final judgment. Far from an empty void, the Astral’s silver substance churns with currents and storms from the metaphysical heat of the [Plane of Fire](/Planes.aspx?ID=31), and where it touches the chaos of the [Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=43), the resulting eddies interact with the memories of the dead to produce fleeting simulacra and even demiplanes.

The River of Souls draws the attention of soul-hunting [daemons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386) and opportunistic [night hags](/Monsters.aspx?ID=257) like sharks drawn to the scent of blood. Led by [psychopomps](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458), a cross section of nearly every type of [celestial](/Traits.aspx?ID=551) and [monitor](/Traits.aspx?ID=655) in existence, along with some [fiends](/Traits.aspx?ID=600), defends the proper flow of souls against such predators. Running opposite the River of Souls is the flow of raw quintessence spun off from the so-called Antipode, channeled by aeons back toward the [Creation’s Forge](/Planes.aspx?ID=27).

Travelers within the Astral find the plane untouched by the passage of time, a property exploited by many mortals fearing old age. Time, however, isn’t easily escaped, and upon exiting the Astral Plane, a creature finds this debt catching up to them, potentially aging to dust in moments.
name: Astral Plane

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Ethereal Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=34)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Subjective Gravity" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=705" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 177

**Category** Transitive Planes

**Divinities** Alazhra

**Native Inhabitants** [ether spiders](/Monsters.aspx?ID=203), [hags](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=425), shades (terrorized)

</column>

---

The Ethereal Plane is a vast, misty realm overlapping each of the [Inner Planes](/Rules.aspx?ID=3022). Formed by the interacting tidal forces of creation and destruction from [Creation’s Forge](/Planes.aspx?ID=27) and [the Void](/Planes.aspx?ID=28), this plane swirls with currents and eddies of fog, lit only by erratic pulses of soft green luminescence and dim light of those planes it overlays, visible but ever intangible. While mortals most often use the Ethereal Plane as a means of transit, moving by force of will in the absence of gravity to bypass barriers on their own plane, the Ethereal hosts dangers and wonders, things lost or abandoned in the mists, and things spun from local eddies in the ethereal protomatter. Predatory monsters, [ether spiders](/Monsters.aspx?ID=203), [hags](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=425) and their goddess Alazhra, and all manner of incorporeal [undead](/Traits.aspx?ID=722) roam the Space Between Spaces.

While travelers can easily become lost in the mists with little to guide them, the plane does host some permanent structures, drawing adventurers or dissuading them. One such location, the House of the Itinerant Soul, houses wayward or lost souls, offering visitors shelter and a way to avoid turning into undead while avoiding the pull of the River of Souls. The grand cathedral also serves as a focus for mortal planar travelers, given its safety and the presence of friendly spirits willing to serve as guides in the surrounding mists.
name: Ethereal Plane

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[First World](/Planes.aspx?ID=35)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Erratic" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=592" />
<trait label="Metamorphic" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=649" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 177

**Category** Transitive Planes

**Divinities** [the Eldest](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=7)

**Native Inhabitants** [fey](/Traits.aspx?ID=599), [linnorms](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=439)

</column>

---

The First World was a first draft of [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), crafted by divinities to test their metaphorical materials and palettes of colors before setting it aside to create a second, final version of their work. A realm of extremes—bestial, primal, and beautiful— with colors and sensations brighter and more intense than the mundane world created after it, the First World is populated by [fey](/Traits.aspx?ID=599) and the divine entities known as the [Eldest](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=7). Mirroring mercurial fey whimsy, the First World’s laws of nature constantly and unpredictably change. Distance and time are wildly inconsistent, such that mortal travelers might spend an hour or a day within the First World, only to find a century or only a few seconds passed once they return to their own plane.

The First World stands outside the cycle of souls, something the fey call the Great Abandonment, save for rare worshippers of the Eldest whose souls incarnate here as fey. However, the plane’s proximity to [Creation’s Forge](/Planes.aspx?ID=27) provides an environment bursting with all manner of strange life and a general absence of true death for its native fey unless they leave—as did [gnomes](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=61). Natural gates in wild places of the mortal realm connect to the First World, which fey often use to visit the Universe or ensnare mortals for their capricious desires.
name: First World

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[The Netherworld](/Planes.aspx?ID=36)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Shadow" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=693" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 178

**Category** Transitive Planes

**Divinities** [velstrac demagogues](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=111), [Zon-Kuthon](/Deities.aspx?ID=296)

**Native Inhabitants** [d'ziriaks](/Monsters.aspx?ID=644), [kayals (fetchlings)](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=30), shades (the mutilated), [umbral dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=179), [velstracs](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=111)

</column>

---

A murky, distorted, and imperfect mirror of [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), the Netherworld overlaps the Universe and serves as a buffer or conduit between it and [the Void](/Planes.aspx?ID=28). The Netherworld exists in a state of perpetually dim half-light, the landscape containing similar features to the overlapping Universe, but in warped or twisted fashions. Cities in the Universe might exist in the Netherworld, sometimes in ruins and sometimes as terrible, frightening replicas. The darkness also holds points of beauty and relative safety, such as the great city of Shadow Absalom. The bleak doppelganger of Golarion’s own Absalom hosts cross-planar trade facilitated by a permanent, one-way portal out of the gloom.

The Netherworld is populated by dark, altered versions of creatures from the Universe, many of them immigrants that have adapted to the shadow after being trapped in the realm for generations. The shadow natives known as [kayals](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=30)—also called fetchlings—were originally [humans](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=64) before thousands of years of exposure to the Netherworld and intermarriage with strange beings forever altered them. [Velstracs](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=111), who long ago fled from [Hell](/Planes.aspx?ID=42), have since adopted the Netherworld as their home, spreading their horrific message of perfection through mutilation in service to their victim and patron, the god [Zon-Kuthon](/Deities.aspx?ID=296). In contrast, [d’ziriaks](/Monsters.aspx?ID=644) originated within the Netherworld itself, as did the fearsome [umbral dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=179) ruling self-crafted fiefdoms within the shadows. Greatest of them is Argrinyxia, who rules over Shadow Absalom.
name: The Netherworld

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Abaddon](/Planes.aspx?ID=37)</title>

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 179

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Ahriman](/Deities.aspx?ID=220), [daemon harbingers](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386), [Fumeiyoshi](/Deities.aspx?ID=305), [Horsemen of the Apocalypse](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=11), [Lao Shu Po](/Deities.aspx?ID=312), [Urgathoa](/Deities.aspx?ID=295), [Zyphus](/Deities.aspx?ID=160)

**Native Inhabitants** [daemons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386), shades (the hunted)

</column>

---

A perpetual eclipse looms above the bleak wastelands of Abaddon, shedding an eerie half-light over a landscape of toxic, disease-ridden swamps, volcanic wastes, fog-shrouded forests, and the glittering, memory-devouring ribbon of the River Styx. An unnatural silence blankets the plane, cut only by the wails of shades falling from the sky like screaming, falling stars, or those already condemned upon the ground, desperate to find safety that doesn’t exist. [Daemons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386), the physical embodiments of meaningless death and oblivion, roam unchecked, owing allegiance only to the [Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=11): [Death](/Deities.aspx?ID=135), [Famine](/Deities.aspx?ID=137), [Pestilence](/Deities.aspx?ID=134), and [War](/Deities.aspx?ID=136). In the courts of the Horsemen and the neutral grounds of trade cities such as Awaiting-Consumption, the soul trade serves daemonic hunger and industrialized extinction. [Hags](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=425) and other creatures ply the trade or make their way along the margins of daemonic society, eager to avoid consumption themselves by the plane’s nihilistic masters.

[Urgathoa](/Deities.aspx?ID=295) and [Zyphus](/Deities.aspx?ID=160) claim divine domains here, their shades granted freedom from predation, yet something far worse glares down upon these gods and the Horseman alike. Abaddon’s perpetual eclipse might be nothing less than the lidded, comatose eye of the Bound Prince, the First Horseman, betrayed and cast down by the Four, forgotten by the cosmos at large, but far too powerful for them to destroy—waiting, watching, and hungering.
name: Abaddon

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Axis](/Planes.aspx?ID=38)</title>

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 179

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Abadar](/Deities.aspx?ID=277), [Brigh](/Deities.aspx?ID=33), [Chaldira](/Deities.aspx?ID=35), halfling pantheon, [Irori](/Deities.aspx?ID=286), [Lissala](/Deities.aspx?ID=156), [Milani](/Deities.aspx?ID=43), [Norgorber](/Deities.aspx?ID=289), primal monitors

**Native Inhabitants** [aeons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=357), shades (remade)

</column>

---

Axis is a realm of pure, absolute order, unhindered by moral concerns. The plane takes the form of a vast, gleaming, perfectly structured city. Built at the base of [Pharasma](/Deities.aspx?ID=290)’s Spire, the Perfect City is a bulwark against the chaos of the [Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=43) and the [Outer Rifts](/Planes.aspx?ID=45), with vast mechanical armies marching forth to explore, define, and pacify an imperfect, unruly universe. Axis is also home to [axiomites](/Monsters.aspx?ID=2792): beings composed of living mathematics and equations. Axiomites continue to work on their organization of the cosmos but increasingly turn their attentions to the planar city of Axis itself; as with any city, Axis requires nonstop maintenance and improvement in order to resist the march of time.

Axis’s natives are far from the only inhabitants of their cosmopolitan realm. [Devils](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=389) and [archons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=365) often visit along with shades, mortal travelers, and smaller numbers of most every other kind of extraplanar being. [Abadar](/Deities.aspx?ID=277) and other deities make their homes in the realm. The empty, former domain of Aroden languishes here, while below the streets lies [Norgorber’s](/Deities.aspx?ID=289) domain, a labyrinthine undercity.
name: Axis

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=39)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Timeless" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=713" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 179

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Achaekek](/Deities.aspx?ID=29), [Groetus](/Deities.aspx?ID=38), [psychopomp ushers](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458), [Pharasma](/Deities.aspx?ID=290)

**Native Inhabitants** shades (the dead), [psychopomps](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458)

</column>

---

The Boneyard spans an impossibly tall and ever-growing spire of gleaming quintessence that rises up into the silver void of the [Astral Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=33). As the destination of the River of Souls, the Boneyard is where the souls of the mortal dead arrive for judgment and for [Pharasma](/Deities.aspx?ID=290) and her psychopomp servitors to direct them to their respective afterlives. Pharasma’s domain is separated into eight courts, each corresponding to one of the other planes of the [Outer Sphere](/Rules.aspx?ID=3024) and collecting the souls due to that plane. Not every soul goes unchallenged, and proxies of gods and planes argue and debate over souls, with final arbitration conducted by Pharasma herself.

While Pharasma rules absolutely within the Boneyard, she isn’t the only divinity there. The demigod [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) ushers dwell within the plane in service to her, and the deity [Achaekek](/Deities.aspx?ID=29) dwells below at the spire’s root. Looming high above as a skull-faced moon waits [Groetus](/Deities.aspx?ID=38), the god of the end times, his orbit slowly decaying in minuscule iterations supposedly counting down to the last days of the cosmos.

Pharasma’s courts and the surrounding Graveyard of Souls are not the only features atop the spire. Beyond the graveyard, the Spirelands manifest environments much like those of the varied worlds of [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), filled with the souls of neutral shades who have nowhere else to go as they are unclaimed by any gods and did nothing in life to suggest any particular ethos.
name: Boneyard

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Elysium](/Planes.aspx?ID=40)</title>

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 179

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Calistria](/Deities.aspx?ID=279), [Cayden Cailean](/Deities.aspx?ID=280), [Desna](/Deities.aspx?ID=281), [elven pantheon](/Deities.aspx?ID=197), [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10), [Gorum](/Deities.aspx?ID=283), giant pantheon, [Kofusachi](/Deities.aspx?ID=309), [Milani](/Deities.aspx?ID=43), [Nocticula](/Deities.aspx?ID=45)

**Native Inhabitants** [azatas](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=368), shades (the chosen)

</column>

---

Verdant, wild, and unrestrained by order, where passion and creation are fostered and rewarded, the plane of Elysium is a place of wild, idealized natural beauty. The so-called Promised Land and its inhabitants represent a wide variety of freely given benevolence, often willing to directly aid visitors but more often serving as inspirations and muses to foster positive change and self-realized success. Elysium’s shades, known as the chosen, appear as idealized versions of their mortal selves, each pursuing their own self-determined actions and finding their unique paths to join the ranks of the plane’s celestials.

[Azatas](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=368)—the plane’s primary denizens—organize into fleeting, competitive courts, each rewarding heroism and creativity above all else. Elysium hosts a number of resident deities, including [Calistria](/Deities.aspx?ID=279) and the [elven pantheon](/Deities.aspx?ID=197), [Cayden Cailean](/Deities.aspx?ID=280), [Desna](/Deities.aspx?ID=281), [Gorum](/Deities.aspx?ID=283), and various [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10). Visitors from across the planes are drawn to Elysium’s Wandering City of Emerald Song, a mobile, impermanent, and ever-changing city of azatas organized by their princess, Anduarine the Muse, filled with art, craft, revelry, song, and trade.
name: Elysium

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Heaven](/Planes.aspx?ID=41)</title>

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 180

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [dwarven pantheon](/Deities.aspx?ID=196), Easivra, [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10), [Erastil](/Deities.aspx?ID=282), [Iomedae](/Deities.aspx?ID=285), [Shizuru](/Deities.aspx?ID=315), [Torag](/Deities.aspx?ID=294), [Tsukiyo](/Deities.aspx?ID=317)

**Native Inhabitants** [archons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=365), shades (the elect)

</column>

---

The great mountain of Heaven is the realm of structured benevolence made manifest. Organized into seven tiers, the mountain’s solid appearance is actually malleable, making way for a vast assemblage of varied environments to accommodate both its own [celestials](/Traits.aspx?ID=551) and the souls who migrate there. Devoted to defending the innocent and crusading against the wicked, Heaven’s [archons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=365) marshal into vast armies, commanded by their [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10). [Angels](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=359) collaborate with the archon legions but typically act in more direct service to Heaven’s resident deities, with [Iomedae](/Deities.aspx?ID=285), [Erastil](/Deities.aspx?ID=282), and [Torag](/Deities.aspx?ID=294) the most prominent among them.

Shades known as the elect manifest at the mountain’s base, and their subsequent climb up the seemingly endless, unreachable heights is both a literal and figurative journey. Progress is about personal growth and spiritual purification as the shades grow more and more attuned to the plane. Likewise, progress from one layer to the next is often impossible without permission from the archons or spiritual alignment with Heaven itself. Most visitors arrive at the city of Heaven’s Shore, a place open to both traders and to pilgrims of good intent. Access beyond its heavily guarded walls is difficult, to say nothing of scaling the mountain. At its pinnacle is the Garden, Heaven’s ultimate layer. Unattended and empty, the Garden is a beautiful, transcendent mystery even to its resident deities and the source of the plane’s call of self-perfection to its shades.
name: Heaven

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Hell](/Planes.aspx?ID=42)</title>

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 180

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [archdevils](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=4), [Asmodeus](/Deities.aspx?ID=278), [Dahak](/Deities.aspx?ID=150), [Erecura](/Deities.aspx?ID=151), [General Susumu](/Deities.aspx?ID=306), infernal dukes, Minderhal, [Yaezhing](/Deities.aspx?ID=318), Zursvaater, [queens of the night](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=14)

**Native Inhabitants** [devils](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=389), [hell hounds](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=427), shades (the damned)

</column>

---

Hell is the realm of devils, the multiversal seat of tyranny and malignant laws, and the divine domain of [Asmodeus, the Prince of Darkness](/Deities.aspx?ID=278). Here, every act is authorized, calculated, recorded, and set like perfectly ordered clockwork within a vast machine driven on methodical suffering and greased with pain and purification. The nine inverted layers of Hell violently oppose the surrounding fabric of the [Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=43), each layer shaped to reflect the nature of its ruling [archdevil](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=4). Avernus’s volcanic wastes, ruled by [Barbatos](/Deities.aspx?ID=64), are the marshaling place of Hell’s armies and where newly damned souls are shackled and shuffled off to their assigned torment. [Dispater](/Deities.aspx?ID=66)’s layer of Dis is a great iron city: brutal, beautiful, and terrible. The layer of Erebus is comprised of both the sewers of Dis and the vaults and treasuries of Hell, ruled by Mammon, a great genius loci embodied by the very wealth locked within Hell’s coffers. Ruled by [Belial](/Deities.aspx?ID=65), the layer of Phlegethon hosts Hell’s forges, while [Geryon](/Deities.aspx?ID=67)’s watery realm of Stygia houses Hell’s libraries. [Moloch](/Deities.aspx?ID=70)’s smoldering, ash-draped forest realm of Malebolge is the training site of the infernal armies, and [Baalzebul](/Deities.aspx?ID=63)’s frozen layer of Cocytus torments the imprisoned, starving damned. [Mephistopheles](/Deities.aspx?ID=69) rules the layer of Caina, a realm of cages and torture suspended above a pit of hungry darkness, while at the very bottom of Hell’s infinite pit, Asmodeus rules unquestioned from his throne in Nessus.
name: Hell

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=43)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Metamorphic" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=649" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 180

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Besmara](/Deities.aspx?ID=32), [Hanspur](/Deities.aspx?ID=154), [Hei Feng](/Deities.aspx?ID=307), [Naderi](/Deities.aspx?ID=157), [Nalinivati](/Deities.aspx?ID=313), [Nethys](/Deities.aspx?ID=288), orc pantheon, protean lords, [Sivanah](/Deities.aspx?ID=46), Speakers of the Depths, [Sun Wukong](/Deities.aspx?ID=316), [Yamatsumi](/Deities.aspx?ID=319), [Ydersius](/Deities.aspx?ID=211)

**Native Inhabitants** [proteans](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=457), shades (the shapeless)

</column>

---

The Maelstrom, infinite and ancient, spawned the other [Outer Planes](/Rules.aspx?ID=3024) in cosmological prehistory and surrounds them like a vast metaphorical—and at times literal—ocean of raw, seething quintessence. Where the Maelstrom borders these other planes, its structure takes on their characteristics, albeit in an unpredictable, chaotic fashion. Beyond these so-called Borderlands, however, the Maelstrom reverts to its true nature, the Cerulean Void: a trackless, liquid infinity devoid of stability and permanence where serpentine [proteans](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=457) create and destroy with profound frivolity. The proteans are paradoxically organized into discrete choruses, each with its own philosophy and goals in service to the Maelstrom.

Swirling with oddities and wild magic, bereft of laws and structure, the Maelstrom also serves as a conduit between the other Outer Planes utilized by armies of extraplanar beings, hordes of [demons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=387) spilling forth from the [Outer Rifts](/Planes.aspx?ID=45), and the innumerable protean choruses seeking to return the rest of the planes to the true freedom of the chaos from which they emerged. Sufficiently powerful magic can stabilize the chaos for a time, allowing for the creation of demiplanes and stable islands. The massive planar trade city of Galisemni is most prominent among them, drifting through the chaos.
name: Maelstrom

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Nirvana](/Planes.aspx?ID=44)</title>

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 180

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** [Daikitsu](/Deities.aspx?ID=304), [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10), [Gruhastha](/Deities.aspx?ID=39), [Kazutal](/Deities.aspx?ID=41), [Kurgess](/Deities.aspx?ID=42), [Qi Zhong](/Deities.aspx?ID=314), [Sarenrae](/Deities.aspx?ID=292), [Shelyn](/Deities.aspx?ID=293)

**Native Inhabitants** [angels](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=359), shades (the cleansed)

</column>

---

The pastoral paradise of Nirvana is the realm of purest benevolence, a plane that promises sanctuary to the weary and enlightenment and transcendence to those who seek it out. Filled with beautiful wilderness of all types in perfect harmony with its occupants, Nirvana’s wilds are home to [angels](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=359) and others. The plane’s shades, known as the cleansed, most often take the forms of glorified, sapient wild animals, though many eventually leave their carefree existence to aid others, ascending to assist the benevolent works of angels. Devoted to guiding and assisting mortals, angels are often charged with delivering important messages that reflect the will of benevolent deities.

Various divinities make their home amid Nirvana’s wilds, including a host of [empyreal lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=10) and, most prominently, [Sarenrae](/Deities.aspx?ID=292) and [Shelyn](/Deities.aspx?ID=293). While the great cities of High Ninshabur and Llourith welcome visitors, most mortals visiting Nirvana witness the plane’s beauty and supernatural feeling of peace but rarely encounter many of its residents, as the plane protectively hides much of its contents from any but the most selfless and pure of heart. The plane’s hinterlands hint at hidden mysteries, including legends that the plane shelters the vanished heroes of innumerable mortal worlds, peacefully sleeping until their peoples’ times of greatest need.
name: Nirvana

<traits>

</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 181

**Category** Outer Sphere Planes

**Divinities** Camazotz, [demon lords](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=5), [Droskar](/Deities.aspx?ID=28), [Ghlaunder](/Deities.aspx?ID=36), goblin hero-gods, [Gyronna](/Deities.aspx?ID=153), [Lady Nanbyo](/Deities.aspx?ID=311), [Lamashtu](/Deities.aspx?ID=287), nascent demon lords, [qlippoth lords](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=460)

**Native Inhabitants** [demons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=387), shades (larvae), [qlippoth](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=460)

</column>

---

Like a corrosive rot in the roots of the [Outer Sphere](/Rules.aspx?ID=3024), or an antithetical, rival reality run aground into the [Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=43) at the dawn of time, the Outer Rifts is a place of horror and destruction fed by mortal sin. Each of its innumerable regions is a unique iteration of chaos and malice, each with its own terrible and twisted environment, with one driving maxim: the strong survive, while the weak suffer and are destroyed. Ruled by demons, and before them by the alien, nightmarish [qlippoth](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=460), the Outer Rifts and its native beings seek only to ruin and destroy. Though [demons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=387) routinely rampage out into other planes when the Outer Rifts manifests a great planar rift, the denizens of the Outer Rifts are fractured and self-destructive. Demon lords such as [Dagon](/Deities.aspx?ID=75), [Pazuzu](/Deities.aspx?ID=79), and [Zura](/Deities.aspx?ID=83), and even gods including the ascended demon lord [Lamashtu, the Mother of Monsters](/Deities.aspx?ID=287), fight for dominion over the Outer Rifts as much or more than they threaten the other planes.

Though dangerous, not every realm of the Outer Rifts is immediately hostile to the prepared traveler. Shamira’s city of Alushinyrra in the Midnight Isles and [Abraxas](/Deities.aspx?ID=71)’s library-city of Diovengia in the realm of [Pleroma](/Monsters.aspx?ID=2811) are each welcoming in their own dark ways. Even those who survive a journey through the Outer Rifts, however, inevitably must resist being deeply corrupted by the experience.
name: Outer Rifts

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Dimension of Time](/Planes.aspx?ID=46)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Erratic" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=592" />
<trait label="Static" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=702" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 181

**Category** Dimensions

**Divinities** unknown

**Native Inhabitants** shades (the unbound)

</column>

---

Among the most obscure planes of existence, the Dimension of Time is virtually impossible to access by conventional methods of planar travel. Instead, travelers must perform complex and difficult rituals described within the pages of monstrously rare tomes. Compounding the plane’s obscurity, each traveler’s experience seems to be unique, and most who attempt the journey never return. Every manner of magic that interacts with the plane invariably draws the attention of creatures native to the dimension. The proper flow and continuity of time seems to self-correct for any attempted meddling, often with violence.

Those few who have gained entry and returned describe the plane as a swirling storm of blurred images, the composite of millions of interwoven timelines around their own, with a single doorway allowing access to one’s own past. Travelers appear as transparent images of themselves, but the plane seems averse to any attempt to alter the past, no matter the means or the intention. Such attempts often end in the offending individual erased from existence or trapped within closed time loops to limit the damage they inflict upon the overall flow of time.

Supposedly, like an eye in a storm, at the dimension’s heart is a legendary realm. Tales of this realm speak only of a great city, a vast green meadow, and the rushing sound of an ocean emptying over an immense and terrible waterfall.
name: Dimension of Time

<title level="1" right="Plane " pfs="">[Dreamlands](/Planes.aspx?ID=47)</title>

<traits>
<trait label="Flowing" url="/Traits.aspx?ID=607" />
</traits>

<column gap="tiny">

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 181

**Category** Dimensions

**Divinities** unknown

**Native Inhabitants** [animate dreams](/Monsters.aspx?ID=546), shades (dreamers)

</column>

---

Created and sustained by the collective dreams of sleeping mortals, the Dreamlands (also called the Dimension of Dreams) overlays the [Ethereal Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=34). When a creature dreams, regardless of the location of their physical body, they interact directly with the Dreamlands. Sleeping creatures cast themselves into the plane in idealized avatars known as lucid bodies, and their dreamscapes are immune to outside entry by standard magic, such as interplanar teleport, requiring obscure, more specialized spells to access. The dreamers of each mortal world generate a cluster of dreamscapes, like drifting bubbles atop a deeper ocean of permanent dreams formed from the collective mass of slumbering desires, dreams of especially powerful dreamers, and the dreams of ancient, obscure entities.

While most dreamscapes are safe, travelers in the deep, permanent Dreamlands face living, animate dreams and the predation of hags from the Ethereal Plane as well as stranger beings spawned within the core itself. Some scholars postulate a distinct region within the dimension for nightmares, much as the more stable inner portions of the dimension exist separately from the transient, ever-forming and evaporating mortal dreamscapes at its edges.
name: Dreamlands

**Source** [Kingmaker Adventure Path](/Sources.aspx?ID=150) pg. 554 **Location** A claimed hex of the GM's choosing but not in the influence of a settlement **Kingdom Skill** [Exploration](/Skills.aspx?ID=23) **Leader** Viceroy --- You hear that a well-preserved ruin in a remote reach of your kingdom may contain historical artifacts connected to people who lived in your land long ago. You can send a team of archaeologists to investigate. **Critical Success** The site proves to be an ancient Kellid settlement that greatly predates the Taldan expansion into the region. Your people are delighted by the discovery. Reduce Unrest by 1d4, and until the end of your next Kingdom turn, all Culture- and Economy-based skill checks gain a +2 circumstance bonus. If any of your settlements has a Museum, you put archaeological findings from this hex on display to the further delight of your citizens. Reduce Unrest by an additional 1.
**Success** The site proves to be remains of an old Taldan camp from the Second Army of Exploration. The discovery is interesting but not particularly surprising. Reduce Unrest by 2.
**Failure** The site proves to be the remains of a relatively recent village abandoned during a previous attempt to settle the Stolen Lands. Nothing particularly interesting is discovered.
**Critical Failure** You never hear from your archaeologists again. Increase Unrest by 1d4 and Decay by 1. You can try to investigate this site again as a [Region](/Traits.aspx?ID=440) activity during the Activity phase of any Kingdom turn by attempting another Exploration check, but as the investigation has proven dangerous, the DC increases by 2.
name: Archaeological Find

**Source** [Kingmaker Adventure Path](/Sources.aspx?ID=150) pg. 554 **Kingdom Skill** [Intrigue](/Skills.aspx?ID=26) **Leader** Warden --- One of your leaders or prominent citizens is targeted by assassins. The GM determines who hired these assassins, then attempts a DC 11 flat check. On a success, the target is an NPC assigned to a leadership role. On a failure (or if the PCs have no NPC leaders), the target is a notable NPC in the kingdom, preferably one with whom the PCs have established a friendly relationship. **Critical Success** The assassins are caught, driven off, or otherwise thwarted without ever endangering their targets. The targeted NPC may not even realize they were in danger, but criminal elements in your nation take notice. Reduce Unrest by 1d4 and reduce one Ruin of your choice by 1.
**Success** The assassins reach their target but are driven off or defeated before the NPC is harmed.
**Failure** The assassins reach their target and manage to harm but not kill the target before they are driven off or defeated. Gain 1d4 Unrest, and if the NPC was a leader, they are removed from that leadership role until the end of the next Kingdom turn.
**Critical Failure** The assassins slay the NPC and escape. If the NPC was a leader, that leadership role is now vacant. Gain 2d4 Unrest and 1d4 points to Decay. --- **Special** If this event occurs while the kingdom is at war, increase the event's level modifier to +3.
name: Assassination Attempt

**Source** [Kingmaker Adventure Path](/Sources.aspx?ID=150) pg. 555 **Kingdom Skill** [Defense](/Skills.aspx?ID=21) **Leader** Warden --- Bandits have begun to prey upon your kingdom! **Critical Success** The bandits are captured, run off, or defeated, and merchants can confidently ply their wares. Reduce Unrest by 1d4, and during the next Kingdom turn, you gain a +1 circumstance bonus to Economy-based checks.
**Success** The bandits are prevented from preying upon your people, but they continue to be active. Your Resource Dice are reduced by 1 at the start of your next Kingdom turn.
**Failure** The bandits plunder caravans and remote storehouses, and merchants grow cautious and afraid. Gain 1 Unrest, and at the start of your next Kingdom turn, your Resource Dice are reduced by 4.
**Critical Failure** Bandits rampage the land, and even start to encroach into your settlements. Gain 1d4 Unrest, increase a Ruin of your choice by 1, and at the start of your next Kingdom turn your Resource Dice are halved or reduced by 4, whichever results in a lower total. --- **Resolution** You can end this continuous event with a critical success on the check. Alternately, if the PCs are eager to seek out the bandits, the GM could build a short side quest to play out their attempt to defeat them.
name: Bandit Activity

For the rest of that turn, item bonuses from structures in the settlement are doubled; this increase can exceed the normal maximum item bonus your structures can provide.
**Success** You gain 2 bonus Resource Dice at the start of the next Kingdom turn. For the remainder of that turn, the item bonus granted by one of the settlement's structures (chosen by the players) is doubled; this increase can exceed the normal maximum item bonus your structures can provide.
**Failure** You gain 1 bonus Resource Die at the start of the next Kingdom turn.
**Critical Failure** The boom is brief before it bursts like a bubble, leaving many merchants jealous and frustrated about not being able to take part. You gain 2 fewer Resource Dice on your next Kingdom turn. --- **Special** Boomtown events create a large amount of unexpected waste and trash. If a boomtown event occurs in a settlement without a dump, the result of the Trade check is worsened one degree.

If the citizens demand a new structure, the GM chooses one as close to the kingdom's level as possible, preferably one that decreases Unrest when built. If the citizens demand a structure be demolished, the GM chooses one that best fits the storyline of the campaign, preferably one whose item bonus or other benefits have been rendered obsolete by newer structures. If you are willing to commit each upcoming [Civic](/Traits.aspx?ID=436) activity granted by this settlement to attempting the requested [Build Structure](/Actions.aspx?ID=1372) or [Demolish](/Actions.aspx?ID=1391) activity until they succeed, you do not attempt the check. **Critical Success** You persuade the citizens to give up their demands.
**Success** You quell the citizens' demands for now, but they are not happy about your refusal to comply. Gain 1 Unrest.
**Failure** The citizens continue their demands and some refuse to work in protest. Gain 1 Unrest and roll 2 fewer Resource dice at the start of your next turn.
**Critical Failure** As failure, but violence during the protests also increases Decay by 1. --- **Resolution** You can end this continuous event by critically succeeding or succeeding at the check or successfully building or demolishing the requested structure.
name: Building Demand

**Source** [Kingmaker Adventure Path](/Sources.aspx?ID=150) pg. 556 **Location** A randomly determined settlement of the GM's choice—the GM should not reveal which one to the PCs until they succeed at this event's [Intrigue](/Skills.aspx?ID=26) check, as until then the location of the cult's hideout should remain a mystery to solve! **Kingdom Skill** [Intrigue](/Skills.aspx?ID=26) **Leader** Magister --- A religious cult opposed to the kingdom's goals or established religions begins kidnapping, converting, or sacrificing citizens. The cult remains secret and hidden in one of the nation's settlements, operating out of a covert temple or safehouse somewhere in the settlement. You must attempt the Intrigue check to locate the cult each Kingdom turn until you succeed in doing so; then, you must attempt the [Folklore](/Skills.aspx?ID=24) check each Kingdom turn until you defeat the cult. **Critical Success** You discover which settlement houses the cult's headquarters. When you attempt Folklore checks to defeat the cult, reduce the DC by 5.
**Success** As success, but without reducing the Folklore DC. Increase Unrest by 1 as a result of the cult's activities.
**Failure** You fail to determine where the cult's headquarters are. Increase Unrest by 1 as a result of the cult's activities.
**Critical Failure** As failure, but increase Unrest by 1d4 and increase Corruption by 1. --- **Resolution** After the cult's headquarters are discovered, to end this continuous event the PCs must defeat the cult by succeeding at the Folklore check. Optionally, the GM can build a short adventure for the PCs to infiltrate the cult headquarters and defeat them in encounter mode. **Special** If this event occurs in a settlement with a Sewer System, you take a –2 item penalty to Intrigue checks to discover the cult's hideout. If this event occurs while the PCs are playing through Chapter 5, increase the event's level modifier to +3. The cult in this case is always associated with the worship of [Lamashtu](/Deities.aspx?ID=11).
name: Cult Activity

**Source** [Kingmaker Adventure Path](/Sources.aspx?ID=150) pg. 557 **Kingdom Skill** [Statecraft](/Skills.aspx?ID=30) **Leader** Emissary --- Another nation or group sends an ambassador to your kingdom. The group sending the overture is randomly selected from the Negotiation DCs sidebar on page 519. If the kingdom has established diplomatic relations with that group, the event proceeds with the Statecraft check below. If the kingdom has not yet established diplomatic relations with the group, this event instead gives the kingdom the opportunity to immediately use the [Send Diplomatic Envoy](/Actions.aspx?ID=1406) Leadership activity. **Critical Success** The diplomatic meeting is incredibly successful, and you can choose two of the following benefits: gain 5 Commodities of your choice, gain an additional 2 Resource Dice at the start of your next turn, or automatically establish a trade agreement with the group.
**Success** As success, but choose only one benefit.
**Failure** Your meeting is cordial but unproductive.
**Critical Failure** Your diplomatic meeting goes poorly. Unless you bribe the group with either 5 Commodities of your choice or 10 RP, they break off diplomatic relations with you. Reestablishing diplomatic relations can be attempted via future Send Diplomatic Envoy activities in the usual manner, but with a –2 circumstance penalty.
name: Diplomatic Overture

**Success** You work to minimize the damage done by the illicit drug trade, but the activity continues. Gain 1 Unrest.

The drug den then moves to another lot chosen by the GM (see Location, above). --- **Resolution** A critical success on the check ends this continuous event. Optionally, the GM can instead build a short adventure for the PCs to infiltrate the drug den and defeat the criminals in encounter mode.

**Success** You satisfy the citizens' demands for growth, but if you don't successfully Claim a Hex during your next Kingdom turn, increase Unrest by 1.
**Failure** Your citizens remain unhappy with your kingdom's growth. Increase Unrest by 1. If you don't successfully Claim a hex during your next Kingdom turn, increase Unrest by 1 again.
**Critical Failure** As failure, but increase the Unrest by 2. --- **Resolution** Achieve either a success or critical success at this event.
name: Expansion Demand
